ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies eye-tracking–derived cognitive features (e.g., first-fixation duration, regression counts, and novel gaze-saliency graph features like edge density and highest-degree fixation weights) to automatic sentiment polarity classification—an application not previously pursued here (claims: “these features are being introduced in NLP tasks like sentiment analysis for the first time”). Prior work cited (Joshi et al. 2014, Mishra et al. 2016) used gaze data for annotation complexity or sarcasm understandability, not direct polarity classification, so the perspective and feature engineering constitute a notable, non-trivial extension rather than a purely incremental combination. Given the reuse of established eye-tracking signals but novel feature formulations and task application, the contribution is clearly differentiated and would be of interest to experts (hence not top-tier novelty but above incremental).

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states and provides experimental results that augmenting traditional features with eye‑tracking (cognitive) features yields up to 3.7% and 9.3% F‑score gains on two datasets, and it describes feature design and datasets (Sections 3–4). However, key methodological details are missing or unclear: train/test/validation splits, cross‑validation procedure, classifier hyperparameters, exact baseline configurations (no comparison to modern neural baselines) and no reported statistical significance tests despite claiming "significant improvement". There are also unsupported/acknowledged assumptions (averaging gaze over participants may lose signal; feasibility claims about large‑scale mobile eye‑tracking are speculative). Given these omissions the experimental claims are plausible but not fully substantiated, so the paper is technically sound but with notable methodological gaps.

CLARITY.justification: The paper generally follows a logical structure (introduction → datasets → feature description → experiments), and the main contribution (augmenting traditional features with cognitive/eye-tracking features) is stated early (section 1.4). However, presentation is marred by inconsistent section numbering and missing subsections (e.g., 1.3 and 4.3 omitted, 4.4 follows 4.2), frequent duplicated/copy‑pasted sentences and repeated citation fragments that clutter the text, and a number of formatting artifacts. Notation and feature abbreviations are mostly defined (e.g., FDUR, SL, RSF), but ordering and labeling errors (feature numbering mismatches) reduce clarity; figure/table references (Figure 2, Table 1) are present but not clearly integrated. Overall the writing is understandable but contains grammatical/formatting issues and needs careful editing for readability and consistency.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper does perform empirical comparisons between models using traditional features and models augmented with cognitive (eye‑tracking) features and reports F‑score gains (max 3.7% and 9.3%) on two datasets (it also evaluates with SVM/NB and a rule‑based system trained on Pang & Lee and Sentiment140). However, it does not present direct comparisons to several important recent/strong baselines (e.g., state‑of‑the‑art deep learning methods it cites like Maas et al. 2011 or dos Santos & Gatti 2014, and it does not clearly compare against prior sarcasm/sentiment systems such as Riloff et al. or Joshi et al. with head‑to‑head results). While the same datasets are used and standard metrics are referenced, the paper lacks explicit, comprehensive baseline bench‑marking and discussion when prior methods perform comparably or better.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates on two public eye-tracking sentiment datasets (Dataset 1: 994 snippets; Dataset 2: 843 snippets), uses multiple classifiers (SVM, Naive Bayes, rule-based) and reports F‑score improvements (up to 3.7% and 9.3%), and states feature‑significance analysis and held‑out experiments — showing reasonably comprehensive experiments and ablation via different feature combinations. Related work coverage is extensive (many SA and eye‑tracking citations) and the paper includes an error analysis and feasibility/limitations section (hardware limits, averaging of participant graphs). However, important experimental details are missing in the provided text (precise train/test splits, cross‑validation, hyperparameters, statistical significance testing, and full result tables/code), which weakens full reproducibility and leaves minor gaps in sensitivity analysis.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important and well-known challenge in sentiment analysis—handling nuanced constructs like sarcasm, irony and thwarting—by introducing cognitive (eye-tracking) features and reporting F‑score gains (up to 3.7% and 9.3% on two datasets) over traditional features (Sections 1.4, 5). The approach is novel and could shift researchers to consider psycholinguistic signals for hard cases (claims of being first of its kind; feature and dataset descriptions in Sections 3–4), and the authors discuss practical deployment paths via mobile eye‑trackers (Section 7). However, the requirement for gaze data constrains immediate broad adoption, limiting transformative impact unless collection becomes routine, so the contribution is of moderate but meaningful significance.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes — this is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it addresses sentiment/sarcasm detection (core NLP tasks) and introduces multimodal/cognitive features (eye-tracking) that are of clear interest to the community. The paper uses public eye‑tracking datasets (Mishra et al. 2016, Joshi et al. 2014), standard evaluation (SVM/NB, held‑out tests, feature significance analysis) and reports quantitative gains, matching expected methods and evaluation standards. The target audience (NLP researchers working on sentiment, sarcasm, multimodal and cognitive signals) is appropriate and the paper is positioned with relevant prior work and feasibility discussion.

REPLICABILITY.justification: Paper lists the datasets (Dataset 1 and 2 with sizes, annotator counts and eyetracker models/sampling rates) and enumerates the full feature set (sentiment, sarcasm, gaze, readability) and tools used (NLTK, MPQA, SentiWordNet, Weka, LibSVM), and it states PCA to 500 components. However, crucial reproducibility details are missing: no model hyperparameters (SVM kernel, C, NB priors), training procedures or splits (how the held-out set was formed, cross-validation), and no preprocessing specifics (tokenization/normalization for tweets, handling of eye-tracking raw signal, fixation/saccade detection parameters or mapping gaze samples to words). The paper also does not provide code or exact evaluation definitions (which F-score variant, significance testing), so reimplementation would require many assumptions.
